title: UK Parliament Launches Formal Inquiry Into Banking Restrictions on Crypto Firms
dek: The Crypto and Digital Assets All-Party Parliamentary Group, chaired by Ed Vaizey and Gurinder Singh Josan, has opened a formal inquiry into bank restrictions on cryptocurrency businesses. The group is inviting written evidence over six weeks before publishing findings and recommendations to the UK government.
author: Crypto Cronkite
key_fact: UK Parliament's cross-party crypto group launched an official inquiry into debanking and payment restrictions imposed by banks on crypto firms, signaling institutional focus on a systemic barrier to sector access.
bottom_line: Watch for the six-week evidence submission period to close and the subsequent APPG report, which will indicate whether the inquiry produces legislative proposals or regulatory recommendations to the Financial Conduct Authority and Bank of England. The story weakens if the report attracts little political follow-up or if banks formally decline to cooperate with the evidence-gathering process.
body: The Crypto and Digital Assets All-Party Parliamentary Group (APPG) opened a formal inquiry on Tuesday into bank restrictions on cryptocurrency and digital asset businesses, according to CoinDesk. The APPG is chaired by Ed Vaizey, a former Minister for the Digital Economy and House of Lords member, and Gurinder Singh Josan, a Labour MP.

The inquiry targets banks that have refused crypto firms bank accounts and introduced restrictions on crypto-related payments, including blocking transfers to certain crypto entities or imposing transaction limits. The group is inviting written evidence from the banking, payments, fintech, and crypto sectors over a six-week submission window. After the evidence period closes, the APPG will publish a report setting out its findings and recommendations to the UK government.

The scope is broad. The inquiry will examine how banking restrictions are being applied, whether they are proportionate, and what impact they have on consumers, businesses, innovation, and competition, per CoinDesk. The APPG will also examine restrictions and access issues affecting professional services such as insurance for crypto businesses.

Ed Vaizey stated: "Over a number of years, the APPG has heard consistent reports from crypto and digital asset businesses that they face difficulties accessing bank accounts and banking services, alongside concerns about restrictions on crypto-related transactions by banks." The pattern mirrors systemic debanking documented in the United States and referred to as "Operation Chokepoint 2.0," according to CoinDesk.

The bear case centers on execution and enforcement. The sources do not explain the mechanism by which the APPG's recommendations would be implemented, what enforcement power Parliament would have over banking practices, or whether banks would cooperate with the inquiry. No bank representatives or regulatory body statements are included in the reporting, leaving their stated justifications for restrictions unclear. CoinDesk also does not specify what outcome crypto businesses expect from the inquiry or whether prior parliamentary inquiries on similar topics have resulted in legislative or regulatory change.
